title: Level 68

{{DISPLAYTITLE:'''Think About the Water Bills'''}} {{CSS|content= .page-header__categories { width: 0px; height: 0px; visibility: hidden }}} {{Blurbackground|file=Level32.JPG|opacity=0.5}} {{Audio|align=center|file=Level68Audio.mp3|volume=2.5|loop=yes|secrettext=Recording of the rendition present on '''Level 68'''.}}
Recording of the rendition present on '''Level 68'''.
[[File:Level32.JPG|center|link=|1300px]]
'''Survival Difficulty: Class 0'''
'''》Safe'''
'''》Secure'''
'''》Devoid of Entities'''
'''Level 68''' is the 69th level of the Backrooms. __NOTOC____noeditsection__ == Description == {{Blurimage|file=Level32.JPG|caption=An image of the room in '''Level 68''', showing the window.}} The restaurant's sprawling emptiness is evident in every corner. Sparse décor stands in stark contrast to the relentless, echoing cacophony that fills the air—an unmistakable signature of '''Level 68'''. Every sound, from a distant drip to the faint creak of settling beams, bounces off bare walls and tile, creating a restless background noise that never truly fades. Chairs are heaped atop tables in awkward, uneven stacks, their legs jutting out at odd angles, frozen in the perpetual pause of a place that always seems to be closing but never quite does. The floor, marred by scattered patches of slick, glistening residue, hints at a recent, hasty cleaning. A careless mop has left streaks that catch the dim light, each patch amplifying the uncomfortable sense of abandonment, as if the staff vanished in the middle of their duties, never to return. Potted plants fill the corners with a desperate attempt at life, but their presence only deepens the unease. The leaves, stiff and alien to the touch, resemble rough polyester or, in some cases, the gritty surface of sandpaper. While polyester is a common material for artificial greenery, these leaves seem to defy even that, crafted instead from a rigid, unyielding spandex. The plants stand perfectly upright, never wilting or collecting dust, their colors too saturated, their veins too sharply defined, as if grown for a world without sunlight or water. The tables on '''Level 68''' are draped in heavy cloths that cling tightly, refusing to be lifted or shifted. The fabric feels dense, almost waxy, and resists even the most determined hands. Atop these tables, chairs are stacked with precision, yet none can be moved—not an inch. They stand as if fused in place, bound by some invisible force. Attempts to budge them fail, no matter the strength or method used. There is no obvious reason for this stubbornness—no mechanical lock, no adhesive, nothing physical or psychological to explain why these objects remain rooted. Yet, they persist, undisturbed and unchanging, as if the act of moving them would violate some unspoken rule of the level itself. On one side of the room, a vast window divided by a grid of thick mullions dominates the wall, drawing the eye. Along its edges, strange ornamentation—neither clearly geometric nor fully organic—winds in intricate loops and whorls. These enigmatic patterns catch the shifting light, casting complex shadows across the floor and onto a vacant stage that sits modestly under the window’s gaze. The interplay of shadow and shape recalls the solemn beauty of cathedrals or long-abandoned churches, where every architectural detail is charged with silent meaning. Here, the effect is haunting, as if the room itself is caught in a moment of reverence, waiting for an audience that will never arrive. {{Blurimage|file=yoooooo68.jpg|caption=Top view of the window.}} Everything about Level 68 hints at abandonment, yet there are no real traces of prior use. Floors glisten as if freshly mopped, chairs remain stacked atop tables, and the entire space feels deserted—an emptiness that lingers in the air and is often mentioned by those who pass through. In a shadowed corner, half-illuminated by distant light, sits a piano. Its keys remain perfectly still, untouched by any hand, yet a somber, looping rendition of Rick Astley’s “Never Gonna Give You Up” plays on, the notes drifting through the silence with no visible source. The melody’s origin remains a mystery, deepening the uncanny stillness of the room. A constant, unnatural glow pours in from the window, bathing the level in perpetual light, though the true source is impossible to find. Facing this ever-bright façade stands a bar, its counter lined with silent, empty barstools. Behind the bar, shelves are bare except for a few glass bottles, each filled with nothing but plain water. The sense of vacancy is palpable, as if the room is waiting for patrons who will never arrive. Next to the bar, a door leads to the level’s only exit: an elevator. The lift looks unremarkable, just like any office building’s, but its destinations are anything but predictable. The buttons, labeled 0 through 9, have no fixed meanings. Pressing the same button twice rarely leads to the same place; one trip might bring a wanderer to Level 3, while the next could end in an entirely different part of the Backrooms. No pattern has ever been found in the connections, and the elevator’s logic remains a mystery. Inside, the elevator is dim and just a bit too quiet. The fluorescent lights overhead emit a faint, flickering hum that grows steadily louder the longer one remains inside. The floor display above the doors cycles through impossible numbers, strange symbols, and, at times, unfamiliar words in unknown languages. The display never settles, even when the elevator is motionless, and the absence of mirrors or any sort of branding leaves the compartment feeling even more anonymous and unwelcoming. {{Blurimage|file=Maiden.png|caption=A photo quickly taken of Level 68.}} Attempts to make sense of the endless light from the window have gone nowhere. The glass is perfectly clear, but nothing waits on the other side—just a flat field of blinding white. Photographs are always ruined by overexposure, regardless of the equipment or settings. Prolonged staring into the glow evokes an odd sense of nostalgia, and some recall fragmented memories that don’t seem to belong to them at all. On the empty stage beneath tangled shadows, not a single prop or piece of furniture can be found. Yet, when silence falls and a recorder is left running, the playback sometimes reveals the faintest hints of an audience—soft applause, a distant cough, the groan of old seats shifting. None of these sounds are audible in person; they exist only in the recordings. Each capture is unique, and some even include brief moments of muffled speech, incomprehensible and distorted. Any attempt to amplify the audio only destroys it further, leaving behind nothing but static and loss. Rooms never feel quite the same when seen again, and doorways along the walls can simply vanish, leaving nothing but stretches of carpet and pillars. Sometimes, footprints left behind end up circling back to places already passed. It’s as if the place isn’t just repeating itself, but quietly folding the halls together, turning wanderers around until even their own tracks can’t be trusted. Farther inside here, you will hear crying, sometimes breaking into broken words that almost seem to echo what’s on the mind rather than anything spoken aloud. Following the sound never leads closer; instead, it draws wanderers deeper into solitude, ending in empty rooms where the only thing left is a lone mirror set upright in the center. === Non-Customer Area === {{Blurimage|file=yoooo6868.jpg|caption=A wide corridor photo.}} By this point, the size of the space returns to homogenization, vastly more spacious than its counterparts. Measuring its boundlessness—a scale long since beyond any notion of immense area—is equally futile. Even before one reaches the point of fading away, the distance to its true extent remains unimaginably remote, and no end can ever be reached. This overwhelming vastness possesses little that stands out. Yet the interior at least offers something more satisfying. It contains change. Spaces of every variety, all fitting their respective themes, can be found here. The countless repetitions that plague other places are present as well, though softened enough to become less noticeable. Beneath the dim, nostalgic glow, answers are only delayed by a few moments, and distinguishing one thing from another remains easy as ever. Edible items can be found here, along with objects suitable for self-defense, both readily discovered by those who make the effort. These supplies sound ordinary enough, yet the former often reaches an almost absurd level of quality. Fish, for instance, are commonly eaten raw immediately after thawing, and no adverse effects have ever been observed afterward. Curious, to say the least. Water often pools across the floor, seemingly imitating the aftermath of rainfall and flooding, as though the drainage systems belonged to a less developed era. Droplets splash against one's feet, leaving the soles slick and unstable. Fortunately, an old mop or a worn rag from a utility closet is usually enough to clear away the grime that might otherwise cause injury. Yellow warning signs are sometimes seen nearby, especially those cautioning about slippery floors. When placed beside the puddles, they appear to cause the water to evaporate unusually quickly. Whether this effect rivals simply cleaning the mess directly is another matter entirely. == Entities == Some believe that a small, yellow, duck-like entity dwells here. It may possess human-level intelligence and remarkable speed, yet it appears harmless to wanderers. It may, perhaps, be a pigeon instead, though no reliable account has yet settled the matter; there are also reports of a dancer. For now, neither has left any trace behind. Thus, this place is provisionally classified as containing no entities. == Phenomena == At times, a wanderer may come upon a menu. Upon its yellowed pages, they will behold things that ought never to be there—countless millennium-era animated pop-up advertisements, as though copied wholesale from the Frontrooms themselves. Though much of their contents are blurred beyond recognition, any eyes not yet damaged can still discern those gaudy, over-saturated displays, overflowing with the names of all things upon Earth. They are arresting, sensational even, and yet so very divorced from reality. Their frames are clad in that most ordinary shade of grey, save for a single crimson circle marked with a symbol of negation—a striking anomaly, standing apart from all else. Should one touch that vivid speck of color, each absurdity, as expected, vanishes at once. There is no means by which to restore it; one could say it is consigned to oblivion. Yet as the seconds pass, that unwanted tedium multiplies ever further, spreading with quiet persistence. And should someone truly desire to decipher the messages within, then, as has already been told, what awaits is but a towering mountain built entirely of falsehood. Worse still, memetic hazards may seep into the mind itself, throwing thought into disarray. Whenever one attempts to think, the signals of reason are interrupted by fragments of long-forgotten internet videos and fashions of ages past. In the end, some can bear it no longer, and depart from this world altogether. Devices such as mobile phones suffer from much the same affliction—only to a far graver extent. To open almost any website or application is to invite upon oneself the heavenly punishment cast by this Level. Yet amidst the deluge, there remains a peculiar refuge: those places devoted to rickrolling the unsuspecting. Under the desperate desire to receive even a fragment of information from the outside, they alone offer sanctuary. It is deception employed against deception—a laughable remedy, perhaps, but one that functions well enough, and in its own strange way, still grants amusement. == Attached Information ==
Looking at grand, lavish restaurants always fills me with awe. Before such magnificent scenes could ever exist, countless efforts and sacrifices must have been made to bring them into being. I rarely step into places like these. I don't want to spend too much money on food, after all. I had two children still at school age; I could never simply ignore them. That was in the past. Once the life I used to have left my side, those concerns no longer seemed to matter. Back then, my only goal was to stay alive long enough to reunite with my family. I kept encouraging myself, believing there would always be a way out. I had endured carrying twins, and I was convinced that challenges like these could never bring me down. The scent of bleach filled my nose, its chemical odor overwhelmingly strong. I often wonder if I'm the only person who would consider it a kind of fragrance. I suppose that, after staying somewhere long enough, one's perception of everything begins to change. Though, speaking of cleanliness, I couldn't honestly say this place was particularly spotless. Lint drifted all around me, hardly something that made for a pleasant experience. Once hunger started to creep in, I went to the kitchen and gathered some ingredients. I had to admit that even the survival supplies were of remarkably high quality, which caught me by surprise. Years of cooking experience made me familiar with the equipment almost instantly, despite not having used any of it for months. I still managed to prepare a rather delicious meal—or at least I thought so. Maybe I could even make it to the finals of MasterChef. I sat down near a seat right beside a large window, saying it was within thirty centimeters of it would hardly be an exaggeration. Aside from some issues with the lighting, the only drawback was how far away the tray return area was. Looking out at the scenery beyond, realistic as it seemed, I was overwhelmed with mixed emotions. I missed home dearly, yet at the same time I thanked heaven that I was finally free to do whatever I wanted, free from all restraints. I had become too relaxed, and I thought it was time to move on to another level. But when I accidentally woke my phone's screen, I was greeted by a loading page. Good lord, I thought I was about to die. Yet, moments later, the display returned to normal as though nothing had happened. I glanced at it and realized that what I was looking at happened to be an introduction to this very place. Suddenly, I felt as though I understood something. The world is full of suffering, yet we can still find joy amidst the pain.
== Entrances and Exits == === Entrances === *'''Level 68''' can be entered from any of [[The Main Nine]] levels, though the means of entrance are seldom documented and appear inconsistent with one another. *Finding a wedding-like ballroom in [[Level 315]] will only send one here if the chairs are stacked on the tables. *Jumping down a hole in [[Level 65]] with a pinkish-like hue will send one to '''Level 68'''. *Finally, anyone who gets food poisoning at [[Level 67]] will also be sent '''Level 68''', too. === Exits === *The elevator can take you anywhere. *[[Level 69|Welcome to the next level.]]
